narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 71.8% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with strong indicators of bar success to maintain high pass rates. For you, a Black male with a high family income background and plans to attend full-time, the model's decision hinged primarily on your early academic performance in law school. The strongest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 1 grades, which placed you in the 2nd decile—well below the typical student's 6th decile rank—suggesting challenges in adapting to the program's rigor right from the start. Next in influence was your Year 3 decile rank of 3rd, again lower than the average 6th decile, indicating that your performance didn't improve enough to signal bar readiness. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, compared to the average of 3.21, also contributed by nudging the prediction further toward failure, as it reflected a history of grades below what the model sees in successful bar takers. While your LSAT score of 35 is close to the average of 37, and your full-time status aligns with most students, these weren't enough to offset the academic concerns. In contrast, typical admitted students show stronger decile rankings and GPAs that pull their failure risk down below 50%. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements—but the model focuses on these patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking tutoring to boost your decile rankings if you reapply, or explore programs with bar prep support tailored for full-time students like you. Building a stronger undergraduate-level record through post-baccalaureate options could also help. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
